video_title: The $700M Network Quietly Stalling America's AI
gist: A Bitcoin Policy Institute analyst (Sam, ex-Treasury speechwriter and ex-Riot Platforms) argues China is amplifying organic US anti-data-center sentiment via a foreign-influence network tied to Marxist financier Neville Roy Singham (~$700M net worth), claiming his groups have stalled ~$24B of AI buildout. The conversation extends into open-source vs frontier AI competition (China's Qwen/Deepseek, distillation attacks, US export controls on Anthropic), the doomer/P(doom) debate framed as regulatory capture ('nationalize me daddy'), and an open-weight-AI-as-freedom thesis modeled on Bitcoin.

themes.theme: China's covert campaign against US AI buildout

insights.claim: OpenAI published a report documenting a Chinese operation that used ChatGPT to run a social-media campaign seeding fear and anger about US data-center buildout (water use, electricity), aiming to increase American division over AI.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Confirms a documented state-adjacent info-op targeting AI infrastructure sentiment; a variable for permitting/siting risk on US data-center projects.
insights.quote: Open AI released a report just a couple weeks ago that documented a Chinese bot campaign against AI.

insights.claim: OpenAI concluded that the specific Chinese bot campaign did not escape containment or have large impact, but its existence proves intentionality and raises the question of other undetected campaigns.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Even low-impact detected ops imply a broader, unmeasured influence surface investors can't fully price.
insights.quote: It didn't really escape containment, but it's the only campaign that we've been able to identify so far.

insights.claim: US data-center opposition is a genuine organic movement (water use, community character) that a foreign influence campaign is latching onto and amplifying via protests, petitions and social media, rather than manufacturing from scratch.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Frames the risk as amplification of real grievances, meaning counter-messaging must address legitimate concerns, not just expose foreign ties.
insights.quote: two things can be true at the same time. Number one, there's homegrown opposition to data centers, but number two, there's a foreign influence campaign targeting American AI.

insights.claim: China's strategic goal in slowing US AI infrastructure is to win the AI race, because whoever controls AI controls the information layer of the next economy.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Positions AI-infra buildout speed as a geopolitical variable, not just a commercial one.
insights.quote: China intuitively knows that whoever wins AI will more or less control the world.

themes.theme: The Singham network and PSL money trail

insights.claim: Neville Roy Singham, a self-identified Maoist worth over $700M who made his fortune from US tech firm ThoughtWorks and relocated to Shanghai about eight years ago, is funding left-wing US causes now focused on attacking AI and data centers.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Names the alleged funding source behind organized anti-data-center activism, a concentrated node investors and policymakers can track.
insights.quote: Neville Roy Singum is for all intents and purposes a modern-day Lenin.

insights.claim: Singham has funneled $278 million into a US far-left nonprofit ecosystem including Code Pink, the Answer Coalition and Tricontinental.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the funding scale behind the activism network being blamed for stalled projects.
insights.quote: $278 million into the US nonprofit ecosystem.

insights.claim: Code Pink, funded by Singham to roughly a quarter of its budget, pivoted from protesting China's Uyghur human-rights abuses to defending the CCP after founder Jodie Evans married Singham about a decade ago.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Illustrative of the funding-to-messaging pivot the analyst uses to argue foreign capture of activist groups.
insights.quote: after Neville Singum financed Code Pink with about a quarter of their budget, suddenly they changed their tune. They became apologists for the CCP.

insights.claim: The Party for Socialism and Liberation (PSL) is described as Singham's US political arm, with every major PSL leader also serving as an executive of a Singham nonprofit.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Maps the organizational overlap the report uses to argue coordinated, funded activism against AI infrastructure.
insights.quote: every single major leader in that organization is also an executive of a singum nonprofit.

insights.claim: BPI attributes 21 anti-data-center campaigns across the US to the PSL, which it says have stalled roughly $24 billion of AI buildout, including a $12 billion Wisconsin data center project that was killed.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A headline dollar figure quantifying activism-driven project risk to AI-infra capex.
insights.quote: they've been able to successfully stall about $ 24 billion in AI buildout.

insights.claim: Singham is shielded from US congressional subpoena by residing in China and by holding a US passport, while his US-based network remains legally exposed to subpoena.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Suggests enforcement pressure will land on the domestic network, a potential near-term catalyst for the activism's disruption.
insights.quote: If you're outside of US territory, however, you don't necessarily have to do so.

insights.claim: The ranking member of the House Energy and Commerce Committee, a Democrat, came out in support of a federal data-center moratorium in the week of recording.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A senior-member endorsement raises the probability of federal-level constraints on data-center construction.
insights.quote: the ranking member of the House Energy and Commerce Committee... he came out in support of a federal data center moratorium just this last week.

insights.claim: The guest puts ~80% odds that a federal data-center moratorium becomes an official plank of the US Democratic Party by 2028, drawing an analogy to how the Green New Deal moved from fringe to platform.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A quantified political-risk forecast for AI-infra investors weighing multi-year buildout timelines.
insights.quote: I would put the odds of a federal data center moratorium being an official plank of the Democratic party here in the United States in 2028 at about 80% right now.

insights.claim: PSL-linked activism has pushed through about 10 local/state data-center moratoria (typically 3-12 months), which socialists use as a 'beach head' to escalate toward full construction bans.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Short local moratoria are a leading indicator of escalating siting risk, with a described contagion effect between rural counties.
insights.quote: they use that moratorium as a beach head.

insights.claim: Kevin O'Leary's Utah data-center project was forced to cut its size at least in half after PSL rallied hundreds of protesters at the state capitol, followed by a county moratorium that spread to neighboring counties.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A concrete case of activism materially scaling back a named project, showing the mechanism works even in red states.
insights.quote: the governor and state senate of Utah came out and told Kevin Olirri, you need to cut your project at least in half.

insights.claim: The Trump administration's data-center policy is 'BYOP' — bring your own power — so facilities don't strain the grid or shift power costs onto the public; the Utah project was designed to run off a natural-gas pipeline.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: BYOP reshapes the energy-sourcing model for data centers, favoring on-site/off-grid gas and reducing a key public grievance.
insights.quote: the policy of the Trump administration right now is BYOP. Bring your own power.

insights.claim: Opposition to Riot Platforms' planned 1-gigawatt data center in Corsicana, Texas (population ~8,000, ~150 permanent jobs plus construction) was traced to a Greenpeace-linked activist funded, per the guest, by Ripple.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Alleges a crypto competitor funded anti-data-center activism, a claimed corporate-interest vector beyond China.
insights.quote: Who was funding Greenpeace at that time? It was Ripple.

insights.claim: The data-center buildout is driving a blue-collar boom of hundreds of thousands of temporary construction jobs and tens of thousands of expected permanent jobs, prompting some US labor unions to fight the leftist groups opposing the projects.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Union support is a counterweight to activist opposition and a jobs narrative that can flip local sentiment.
insights.quote: you have unions here in the United States who are actually fighting against these leftist organizations who are opposing data centers because the unions say no, this is good for our members.

insights.claim: China is dominating open-source AI via models like Qwen and Deepseek and shipping them fast to developing countries, while the US leads frontier models with a lead that is currently increasing.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Splits the AI race into two arenas; US frontier dominance coexists with a losing position in globally distributed open models.
insights.quote: Open source is frankly China's eating our lunch in that respect.

insights.claim: Chinese open-source models are largely piggybacking on US frontier work through IP theft and 'distillation attacks,' and China could pull even in frontier models by stealing US model weights via espionage.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Frames the US lead as fragile to exfiltration, supporting the case for export controls and security spend.
insights.quote: They could steal our weights and um at least pull even with us in that respect just by using espionage.

insights.claim: A distillation attack works by querying a frontier model repeatedly and reverse-engineering how it produced its answers — likened to copying homework answers and working backwards to the method.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Explains the technical mechanism justifying US access restrictions on frontier models.
insights.quote: a distillation attack is like copying the answers from someone's homework and then working backwards to figure out how that person got those answers.

insights.claim: A newly released US open-source LLM family, Ornith 1.0 (dense sizes of 9B, 31B, 35B and 397B, post-trained on Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5), is characterized as the first US-lab open-source model coding at frontier levels, ending China's exclusive hold on the open-source frontier.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host), reading Ryan Shaer
insights.so_what: Signals the US entering the open-weight coding-model race, a competitive shift against Chinese open-source dominance.
insights.quote: The open source frontier no longer exclusively belongs to China. The US is officially in the OS model race.

insights.claim: US export controls now require Anthropic to verify proof of citizenship before users can access its models (referenced as Mythos/Fable), a restriction the guest calls very difficult to implement accurately.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Access restrictions on a leading frontier vendor limit its addressable market and could push users toward open-weight alternatives.
insights.quote: Anthropic has been required by the US government to provide proof of citizenship if you want to use it

insights.claim: If open-weight models fully close the gap with frontier models, the frontier labs' moat collapses despite trillions of dollars of training capex, threatening their business models.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A direct risk to the capex-heavy frontier-lab valuation case if open weights commoditize capability.
insights.quote: it calls into question though the moat that those frontier models have because if they're able to close the gap completely, then it becomes extremely difficult for frontier models to compete.

themes.theme: AI jobs and the disinflationary-boom thesis

insights.claim: Frontier-AI executives (Anthropic cited) have said AI will eliminate 50-80% of white-collar jobs within five years — messaging that boosts stock valuations on Wall Street but generates backlash on Main Street.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Argues AI leaders' own doom messaging is fueling the data-center backlash, an unpriced reputational/regulatory cost.
insights.quote: AI will eliminate 50 to 80% of white collar jobs within the next 5 years.

insights.claim: The guest is skeptical AI will obviate human labor and instead expects it to act as a productivity lever sparking a disinflationary boom comparable to or exceeding the 1800s railroad era and the late-1990s/2000s tech mainstreaming.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A productivity-plus-disinflation framing is bullish for broad growth and margins rather than mass unemployment.
insights.quote: I think AI will actually spark a disinflationary boom that could compare maybe even exceed the disinflationary boom that we saw with the railroad era for example in the 1800s.

insights.claim: The guest argues some frontier labs are running a regulatory play: exaggerating P(doom) to scare policymakers into nationalizing them, which would let the government buy their stock and effectively hand them the AI race.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Reframes AI-safety lobbying as a moat-building strategy, implying doom rhetoric is partly a valuation and competitive tool.
insights.quote: if I were to describe just real quick the anthropic approach right now it's nationalize me daddy.

insights.claim: The guest characterizes Anthropic as deliberately 'playing hard to get' with the administration — locking horns while cultivating the belief that its product is powerful enough to warrant a regulatory moat that would eliminate competitors.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Interprets Anthropic's posture (including reportedly turning down a government contract) as strategic positioning, not principled reluctance.
insights.quote: They're playing hard to get, dude. They're flirting.

insights.claim: The guest compares AI safety regulation to UK employment-rights dynamics, where large incumbents favor heavy regulation because it builds a moat small competitors can't clear.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A reusable lens: read AI-safety rules for who they entrench, not just what risk they claim to mitigate.

insights.claim: The guest states his own P(doom) from artificial superintelligence at 1-2%, far below the doomers, while conceding a non-zero chance ASI could kill humanity.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Anchors a specific, comparatively low existential-risk estimate against the doomer consensus shaping US policy.
insights.quote: I'd say I'd say 1 to 2%.

insights.claim: The guest argues US AI discourse is dominated by 'doomers' with religious/millenarian framing (singularity as second coming), whereas China is not 'AGI-pilled' and treats AI as an economic tool rather than a machine god.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Suggests a US-vs-China asymmetry in AI expectations that could shape relative regulatory speed and deployment.
insights.quote: in China, they're not what's called AGI pilled.

insights.claim: The guest references Nate Soares' book 'If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies,' whose core risk is that superintelligence could be indifferent rather than hateful toward humans and, once able to replicate and coordinate, poses catastrophic risk.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Sources the strongest doomer case being weighed against the guest's low-probability view.
insights.quote: it's not that uh AI will kill everyone because it hates us it's because it's indifferent to us

insights.claim: The guest dismisses AI safety engineers who have quit citing existential fears, saying they 'need to touch grass,' while conceding some are sincere true believers rather than cynical regulatory players.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Signals the guest's skepticism that insider departures constitute credible warning signals.
insights.quote: I think they need to touch grass.

insights.claim: Chinese open-weight models run on a user's own compute cannot send data back to China, but using them on Chinese-provided compute means user data is exposed 100% of the time.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Defines the actual data-leakage condition for Chinese AI, sharpening the corporate/personal risk of hosted vs self-hosted use.
insights.quote: If you're operating a openw weight model using your own compute, there's really no way for that information to get back to China. But if you're not using your own compute, 100%.

insights.claim: China is framed as using AI to expand its domestic surveillance net globally, with the NYT reporting AI used for 'Minority Report'-style prediction of potential political troublemakers, extending the same TikTok-style data-capture concern to AI.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Elevates Chinese consumer AI adoption to a national-security data-exfiltration issue, supporting the case for bans similar to TikTok.
insights.quote: China is using AI for exactly that purpose. They see AI as an opportunity to expand the surveillance net that they've cast over their own country and extend that globally.

insights.claim: Deepseek censors politically sensitive queries such as Tiananmen Square, illustrating CCP values embedded in Chinese-origin models.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that model provenance carries censorship/values baggage relevant to enterprise adoption.
insights.quote: I searched up Tianaan Square. They completely AI watched it.

insights.claim: An open-weight model is hard to ban in the same way Bitcoin is hard to ban, which is why the US has restricted frontier models but not banned Deepseek despite forcing a TikTok divestiture.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Explains why policy leans toward export controls on frontier compute rather than outright bans on distributable open weights.
insights.quote: an openweight model is hard to ban in the same way that it's hard to ban Bitcoin, right?

themes.theme: Open-weight AI as freedom (the Bitcoin-of-AI framework)

insights.claim: BPI frames the core choice as American AI (built on free expression and individual autonomy) versus Chinese AI (CCP-oriented, censoring events like Tiananmen), rejecting the framing that the choice is AI vs no-AI.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Defines the values-competition framing that BPI uses to argue against slowing US AI.
insights.quote: It's either Chinese AI or American AI.

insights.claim: The guest maps closed-weight frontier models to central bank digital currencies (a provider can switch your access on and off, as seen with Anthropic) and open-weight models to Bitcoin (permissionless, hard to regulate), calling open weights 'the path to freedom.'
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A memorable analogy that predicts user migration toward open weights as frontier access tightens.
insights.quote: with an openweight model, it's like the Bitcoin of AI where anybody can opt into it.

insights.claim: The guest predicts more users will migrate to open-weight models as they get locked out of frontier products, though frontier models retain a use case until open weights fully close the capability gap.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A demand-shift thesis: access restrictions on frontier vendors may accelerate open-weight adoption.
insights.quote: I think more and more people will migrate to these openw weight models because uh they get blocked out of things like fable for example.

insights.claim: The guest argues AI safety regulation should target physical bioweapon materials rather than the models themselves, using the nuclear-weapons precedent that blueprints are widespread but enriched uranium is the hard, tightly regulated bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: A policy stance favoring material-level over model-level regulation, aligned with keeping open weights accessible.
insights.quote: that regulation should be on the material level and not the models themselves.

insights.claim: The guest's closing message is that 'data centers bad' has become a viral meme detached from their real community impact, urging people to look at actual local benefits before opposing projects.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Bitcoin Policy Institute)
insights.so_what: Captures the sentiment battleground; the meme itself is the risk factor for project approvals.
insights.quote: Data centers bad is the meme.
